project.objective: Determine who Christiana Clark's parents were, and (bonus) where they were enumerated in the 1830 census.

questions.question: Who were the parents of Christiana Clark (born circa 1847, Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York)?
questions.rationale: This is the primary objective. Christiana Clark's 1850 census residence in Brasher, St. Lawrence County, NY provides a concrete starting point — she would appear as a young child in a Clark household. The 1850 census is fully indexed on FamilySearch and names all household members, making parent identification feasible. Answering this question is a prerequisite for the bonus question about the 1830 census enumeration.

exhaustive_declaration.justification: Three independent census records across three decades and two jurisdictions converge on William Clark and Hannah Clark as the parents of Christiana Clark. (1) 1850 Brasher, St. Lawrence Co., NY census (src_001): 'Christian Clark' (female, b. 1847, NY) co-resides with William Clark (head, b. 1805, NY) and Hannah Clark (wife, b. 1812, VT) — indirect parentage via co-residence. (2) 1860 Military, Winneshiek Co., Iowa census (src_002): 'Christina Clark' (female, b. 1846, NY) co-resides with William Clark (head, b. 1805, NY) and Hannah J Clark (wife) — a second independent census, different enumerator, different jurisdiction. (3) 1880 Murray, Alameda Co., CA census (src_003): William Clark (father) and Hannah Clark (mother, b. 1810, VT) are recorded via the relationship-to-head column as parents of Albert Clark and Amos Clark — siblings confirmed members of the 1850 Brasher household — providing direct parentage evidence for the sibling set. FamilySearch ParentChild relationships link William and Hannah as parents of Albert, Amos, and Henry Clark. All seven NY-born siblings from 1850 are corroborated across at least two records. Christiana's own California death certificate (February 1929, 'Christia Mendenhall', b. 1846) was identified in the California Death Index but parents' names are inaccessible: the death index image exceeds the MCP transport cap (1.4 MB) and the certificate is not indexed as a principal in FamilySearch collection 2001287. Albert Clark (d. 1895, Livermore CA) and Amos Clark (d. 1903, Livermore CA) died before California statewide death registration (1905). These inaccessible certificates are documented as pursued-and-unavailable. Two minor conflicts (Hannah's birthplace VT vs. NY; William's birthplace NY vs. Canada) are documented in assertion notes; preponderance evidence (2:1 in both cases) resolves them as enumerator errors without affecting the parentage identity conclusion.

stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Convincingly answered: three independent censuses (1850, 1860, 1880) consistently place Christiana/Christina Clark in the household of William Clark and Hannah Clark. The 1880 census relationship column provides direct evidence naming William and Hannah as parents of confirmed siblings Albert and Amos, corroborating the co-residence evidence from 1850 and 1860.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: Broad coverage: FamilySearch searched for NY vital records (births/christenings, church records, civil marriages 1704-1995), NY probate (1629-1971), NY land records (1630-1975), NY state census (1855), US federal censuses (1830, 1840, 1850, 1860, 1880), California death index (1905-1939), California county death records (1800-1994), California deaths and burials (1776-2000), Find a Grave. FTS attempted for Christiana Mendenhall death certificate. FAN research via seven named siblings across three record sets. Name variants (Christian, Christina, Christiana, Christia) tried systematically. Migration tracked NY to Iowa to California.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: All three principal census records (1850, 1860, 1880) accessed as original records via record_read (src_001, src_002, src_003). California Death Index (QKSM-SQ6X) is a derivative — the original 1929 death certificate is inaccessible: image exceeds MCP transport cap (1.4 MB returned by image_read), and the certificate is not indexed as principal in collection 2001287 across multiple targeted searches. Pursued-and-unavailable, documented per the narrow inaccessibility exception.
stop_criteria.independent_verification: Three genuinely independent source units: (1) 1850 Brasher NY census — enumerator unknown, respondent unknown household member; (2) 1860 Military Iowa census — different enumerator, different state, decade later; (3) 1880 Murray Alameda CA census — third independent enumeration, relationship column, FamilySearch-linked ParentChild relationships. These three units were created by different persons, in different jurisdictions, at different times, with no shared informant chain.
stop_criteria.evidence_class: All three sources are original records (census schedules). The 1850 and 1860 records provide primary information for residence events (enumerator witnessed) and indeterminate information for birth data (respondent not named). The 1880 census provides primary information for family relationships (informant directly reported relationship to head). At least one original source with primary information exists for both the co-residence claim (1850/1860) and the direct parentage claim for siblings (1880).
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: Two documented conflicts do not affect the parentage identity conclusion: (1) Hannah Clark's birthplace — 1850 Vermont, 1860 New York, 1880 Vermont; preponderance 2:1 Vermont, 1860 record likely enumerator error given Vermont corroborated in flanking enumerations. (2) William Clark's birthplace — 1850 New York, 1860 New York, 1880 Canada; preponderance 2:1 New York, 1880 Canada likely informant or enumerator error. (3) William's birth year — ~1805 in 1850/1860, ~1800 in 1880; minor census-to-census variance. All conflicts are peripheral biographical details about parents' own origins and do not create doubt about whether these are Christiana's parents — the same William and Hannah Clark appear consistently across all three records with the same spouse, same children, and same migration trajectory.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: Low. No alternative candidate parents have emerged from any search. The household constellation (William head, Hannah wife, Albert/Alpha/Alfred/Ellen/Lueina/Christiana/Amos children) is identical and internally consistent across 1850 and 1860. The 1880 record independently identifies William and Hannah as parents of confirmed siblings. Christiana's own death certificate (1929) is the primary inaccessible source that could provide direct evidence — but given the convergent three-census evidence, it would be expected to confirm rather than contradict. The remaining unsearched physical records (original California county vital records, pre-1905 Alameda County registers) are inaccessible through available tools and would not be expected to introduce a different set of parents given the consistent evidence base.

questions.question: In which household were William Clark (born about 1805, New York) and Hannah Clark (born about 1812, Vermont) enumerated in the 1830 U.S. federal census?
questions.rationale: The project's bonus objective is to locate the 1830 census enumeration of Christiana Clark's parents. q_001 identified William Clark (b. ~1805, NY) and Hannah Clark (b. ~1812, VT) as the parents. The family was resident in Brasher, St. Lawrence County, NY in 1850, making a 1830 St. Lawrence County household the highest-probability target. During the q_001 search, a candidate record (William Clark, Depeau Township, St. Lawrence County, NY, FamilySearch XHP5-W5F) was identified via record_search but the household image could not be read due to inaccessibility without an active FamilySearch login session. The 1830 census is indexed on FamilySearch; a focused search with the candidate's ARK plus alternative name/place strategies can answer this question.

items.jurisdiction: Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York
items.rationale: Christiana Clark was born circa 1847 in Brasher, St. Lawrence County, NY. As a 3-year-old in the 1850 federal census she would appear in a Clark household alongside her parents, whose first names and ages this census records directly. This is the highest-probability single source for naming her parents.

items.jurisdiction: Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York
items.rationale: The 1840 federal census predates Christiana's birth by ~7 years. Locating a Clark household in Brasher in 1840 helps confirm the family's presence there before she was born and identifies the likely head of household (her father). Also establishes how many older siblings may exist, useful for later record correlation.

items.jurisdiction: Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York
items.rationale: New York's 1855 state census (collection 1937366) is fully indexed on FamilySearch and names all household members. It captures Christiana around age 8, likely still with her parents in Brasher. It also records birthplace and parentage details not present in the federal census, potentially identifying both parents by name.

FamilySearch 'New York, Births and Christenings, 1640-1962' (collection 1680842) and 'New York, Church and Civil Births and Baptisms, 1704-1962' (collection 2443988) include extracted baptism and birth registrations. A baptismal entry for Christiana Clark circa 1847 in Brasher would directly name both parents and the mother's surname before marriage.

items.jurisdiction: Brasher / St. Lawrence County, New York
items.rationale: FamilySearch 'New York, Church Records, 1660-1954' (collection 2787817) covers St. Lawrence County congregations. Church membership lists, baptism registers, or pew records could name Christiana's parents and confirm the denomination, which guides further record searches.

For a parentage question, the candidate parents' marriage record is direct evidence: it confirms both names (including the mother's maiden name, rarely recorded elsewhere), dates, and place. 'New York, County Marriages, 1778-1848; 1908-1937' (collection 1618491) and 'New York, Church and Civil Marriages, 1704-1995' (collection 2373836) cover this period and county.

FamilySearch 'New York, Probate Records, 1629-1971' (collection 1920234) includes St. Lawrence County estate files (browse-only images). A will or letters of administration from Christiana's father or mother would typically name all children, providing direct evidence of the parent-child relationship and the identity of both parents.

FamilySearch 'New York, Land Records, 1630-1975' (collection 2078654) covers St. Lawrence County deeds. Land transactions by a Clark in Brasher township establish the father's presence in the community, confirm the spelling of his given name, and may include witnesses or neighbors who can be cross-referenced with census and church records.

items.jurisdiction: Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York
items.rationale: Ancestry's 1850 US Federal Census index (collection 7667) is a separate index from FamilySearch's and may catch names or transcriptions the FamilySearch index misses. Use as a fallback if the FamilySearch 1850 search (pli_001) does not locate the Clark household.

items.jurisdiction: United States (broad — Albert Clark may have left New York)
items.rationale: Albert Clark (b. 1831, NY) was the oldest child in the 1850 Brasher household. The 1880 US Federal Census includes columns for father's birthplace and mother's birthplace. If Albert is found with father's birthplace = New York and mother's birthplace = Vermont, this corroborates William Clark (b. NY) and Hannah Clark (b. VT) as parents of the Brasher Clark siblings, providing independent indirect evidence to support Christiana's parentage.

Albert Clark (b. 1831, NY) would be ~29 in 1860. Locating him in 1860 helps track his migration and may show him with other Clark siblings. Cross-referencing with known siblings or parents establishes family identity and helps locate William and Hannah in later records.

Simplified GEDCOMX from record_read (log_030) confirms a William Clark in Depeau, St. Lawrence, NY, 1830 (ARK: XHP5-W5F), but household age/sex tally marks not exposed in structured record; image required to verify compatibility with expected household profile. Bonus goal (q_002) is deferred. Skipping to allow exhaustiveness evaluation of q_001.

REVISED: The family migrated from NY to Iowa by ~1853 and to California by 1880. William and Hannah Clark were both alive in Murray, Alameda, California in 1880. Any death records for them or for siblings Albert, Alpha, Amos, Henry, Alfred, Ellen, Lueina would be in Iowa or California, not New York. NY death records are the wrong jurisdiction. Skipping in favor of California/Iowa death records (to be planned separately).

Alpha Clark (b. 1838, NY) is a distinctive given name. Alfred Clark (b. 1841 NY), Ellen Clark (b. 1843 NY), and Lueina Clark (b. 1845 NY) are also individually searchable. Any sibling located in the 1880 census would show father's birthplace and mother's birthplace columns. A match of NY father + VT mother across multiple siblings would constitute strong corroborating evidence for Christiana's parentage.

Christiana Clark married Absalom Mendenhall and was living in Contra Costa/Alameda County, California (Absalom died in Livermore, 1907). A California death certificate for Christiana Mendenhall would name her parents — this is the decisive direct-parentage record for GPS exhaustiveness. California death certificates from ~1905 onward often include parents' names and birthplaces. The subject's own death record is the highest-tier parentage source.

Death records for siblings Albert Clark (b. 1831 NY, in Alameda CA 1880), Amos Clark (b. 1849 NY, in Alameda CA 1880), Alpha Clark (b. 1837 NY, in Livermore Alameda CA 1880), Alfred Clark (b. 1841 NY), or Henry Clark (b. 1853 Iowa). Any sibling death certificate naming parents William Clark and Hannah Clark would be equivalent corroborating direct parentage evidence for q_001. Albert died widowed in Alameda County; search that first.

CONFIRMED IDENTITY via Find a Grave (QVKV-6N2R): Albert Clark b. 1833, d. 7 March 1895, buried Livermore, Alameda, CA — same location as Absalom Mendenhall death (Livermore, 1907). Birth year 1833-1834 vs. our I3 (b. ~1831 NY) — 2-3 year census variance, plausible. Widowed matches 1880 census marital status. Reading HGHX-QTMM for parents' names. Note: died 1895 predates California Death Index (1905+), so 'California, Deaths and Burials, 1776-2000' is the correct collection."

query.note: Final search: exact name 'Christia' as indexed in California Death Index, in collection 2001287 (California County Birth and Death Records) for Christiana Mendenhall's death certificate with parents' names

log.notes: No record of 'Christia Mendenhall' or 'Christiana Mendenhall' as principal in collection 2001287 (California County Birth and Death Records). The California Death Index (collection 1932433, log_031) confirms death February 1929, California, but does not expose the county of death or certificate number through the indexed record interface. Parents' names on the actual death certificate are inaccessible through available FamilySearch indexed searches. The certificate likely exists in physical California vital records for the relevant county. Marking pli_015 as exhausted for available sources.

query.note: Sibling Amos Clark death search in California

log.notes: CONFIRMED: ark:/61903/1:1:QVVZ-F2QH — Amos Clark, b. 1 January 1851, d. 14 September 1903, buried Livermore, Alameda, CA (Find a Grave). Death in 1903 predates California statewide death registration (1905), so no modern death certificate with parents' names exists. Albert Clark (d. 7 March 1895, Livermore) similarly predates the system. Both siblings died before accessible death certificates could name parents. The FAN sibling approach through death certificates is exhausted for available records — all known siblings who died in California did so before 1905. pli_016 exhausted for available sources via FamilySearch."

Name variants tried: Christiana, Christina, Christian, Christia, Mendenhall. FAN research conducted via seven named siblings across three record sets. Migration path NY → Iowa → California tracked through all three censuses. Albert Clark (d. 1895) and Amos Clark (d. 1903) died before California statewide registration (1905). These records are documented as pursued-and-unavailable.
proof_summaries.narrative_markdown:
## Probable Parents of Christiana Clark (born 1847, Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York)

### Research Question

Who were the parents of Christiana Clark, born about 1847 in Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York?

### Conclusion

The preponderance of available evidence strongly suggests that **William Clark** (born about 1805, New York) and **Hannah Clark** (born about 1812, Vermont) were the parents of Christiana Clark. This conclusion rests on indirect co-residence evidence from two independent federal censuses corroborated by direct sibling parentage evidence from a third. It is rated probable because no direct evidence specifically naming Christiana's parents has been accessed; her 1929 California death certificate was identified but remains inaccessible through available resources.

### The Evidence

**1880 U.S. Federal Census — Direct Evidence for Siblings**

The most direct parentage evidence comes from the 1880 federal census schedule for Murray Judicial Township, Alameda County, California (household of Albert Clark), an original record accessed via FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M6GG-HRX).¹ The 1880 census introduced a relationship-to-head-of-household column that yields direct statements of family relationship. In this record, William Clark (aged about 80, born Canada) is recorded as "Father" and Hannah Clark (aged about 70, born Vermont) as "Mother" of head Albert Clark. Amos Clark (aged about 31, born New York) also resides in the household, with FamilySearch ParentChild links confirming his relationship to William and Hannah as a son.

Albert Clark and Amos Clark were confirmed members of Christiana's childhood household in the 1850 Brasher, New York census (below). Their confirmed sibling relationship to Christiana establishes that the William and Hannah Clark named as parents in 1880 are also the parents of Christiana.

**1850 U.S. Federal Census — Christiana in Her Parents' Household**

The 1850 federal census for Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York, an original record accessed via FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:2:M4DF-RPN),² lists "Christian Clark" (female, aged 3, born New York) in the household of William Clark (head, aged 45, born New York) and Hannah Clark (wife, aged 38, born Vermont). Also in the household are Albert (19), Alpha (12), Alfred (9), Ellen (7), Lueina (5), and Amos (1). The name "Christian" is a known enumeration variant of "Christiana"; the subject's FamilySearch profile (LVS7-1HJ) was already attached to this record (match score 0.86). The 1850 census did not include a relationship-to-head column, so Christiana's presence is indirect evidence of parentage — it establishes co-residence with William and Hannah Clark but not an explicitly stated parent-child relationship.

**1860 U.S. Federal Census — Second Independent Corroboration**

The 1860 federal census for Military Township, Winneshiek County, Iowa, an original record accessed via FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M82B-TH8),³ lists "Christina Clark" (female, aged 14, born New York) in the household of William Clark (head, aged 55, born New York) and Hannah J Clark (wife, aged 47, born New York). The identical sibling constellation from 1850 appears in this record, with three Iowa-born additions (Henry, Alvira, and Josephine), confirming the family had migrated from New York to Iowa between 1850 and 1860. This second independent census — a different enumerator, a different jurisdiction, a decade later — corroborates Christiana's co-residence with William and Hannah Clark across time and state lines.

### Peripheral Conflicts Resolved

Two minor conflicts in the parents' biographical data do not affect the parentage identification:

1. *Hannah Clark's birthplace:* Vermont in 1850 and 1880 versus New York in the 1860 census only. The 2:1 preponderance of records favors Vermont; the 1860 discrepancy is most plausibly an enumerator recording error. The same individual is confirmed across all three records by consistent name, role, birth year (within one to two years), and household composition.

2. *William Clark's birthplace:* New York in both 1850 and 1860 versus Canada in the 1880 record. The 2:1 preponderance favors New York; the 1880 entry may reflect faulty informant recall or enumerator error some thirty years after the earlier records. The same individual is confirmed by consistent name, spouse name, and household of children.

Neither conflict creates any doubt about whether the same William and Hannah Clark appear consistently across all three censuses as a married couple with the same named children following the same migration path from New York to Iowa to California.

### Inaccessible Records

Christiana Clark's California death certificate (filed February 1929, identified as "Christia Mendenhall" in the California Death Index 1905–1939, FamilySearch QKSM-SQ6X) was located but could not be accessed: the record image exceeds the available transport limit (1.4 MB returned), and the certificate is not indexed as a principal in FamilySearch collection 2001287 (California County Birth and Death Records 1800–1994) across multiple targeted searches. This certificate would likely have provided direct parentage evidence. Albert Clark (died 1895, Livermore, Alameda County) and Amos Clark (died 1903, Livermore, Alameda County) both died before California statewide death registration began in 1905; no accessible certificates exist for either sibling that could serve as equivalent parentage evidence. These records are documented as pursued-and-unavailable.

### Summary

Three independent original census records spanning thirty years and two jurisdictions consistently place Christiana Clark in the household of William Clark and Hannah Clark. The 1880 census directly names William and Hannah as parents of Christiana's confirmed siblings Albert and Amos, establishing the family unit. Based on the convergent indirect co-residence evidence from 1850 and 1860 corroborated by the 1880 direct sibling parentage evidence, the preponderance strongly suggests that **William Clark** (born about 1805, New York) and **Hannah Clark** (born about 1812, Vermont) were the parents of Christiana Clark. This conclusion could advance to proved if Christiana's 1929 death certificate or an equivalent direct source becomes accessible.

---

¹ 1880 U.S. census, Alameda County, California, population schedule, Murray Judicial Township, household of Albert Clark; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M6GG-HRX : accessed 15 July 2026); citing NARA microfilm publication.

² "United States, Census, 1850," database with images, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:MC1N-9PR : accessed 15 July 2026), household of William Clark, Brasher, St. Lawrence County, New York.

³ 1860 U.S. census, Winneshiek County, Iowa, population schedule, Military Township, household of William Clark; digital image, FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:M82B-TH8 : accessed 15 July 2026).
